statement: Maintaining per-(run_id, stage) incremental counters via transactional state transitions will reduce repeated request scans and preserve correct stage advancement semantics.
rationale: Current orchestration repeatedly recomputes progress from scans of samples/score units and status-partitioned llm_requests. Convex guidance favors denormalized counts and indexed lookups, and Convex mutations provide serializable transactional updates suitable for counter maintenance.
keyAssumptions: State transitions for each target/request can be normalized into a small finite-state machine.
Counter updates and primary entity updates occur in the same mutation to avoid drift.
OCC retry overhead on shared counters remains acceptable for expected throughput.
validationCriteria: `getStageProgress` no longer scans `llm_requests` by status for normal operation.
Stage advancement decisions match baseline behavior on existing orchestrator tests.
At run sizes >= current p95, orchestration mutations show lower read volume and lower latency than scan-based baseline.
A reconciliation job can detect and repair intentional drift injections.
critiquePoints: Hot-counter contention could increase OCC retries for large parallel runs.
Dual-write migration period may add complexity and temporary inconsistency risk.
Counter logic can become brittle if transition edges are not exhaustively modeled.
questions: What is the expected upper bound of concurrent completions per run stage?
Should counters track only terminal buckets (completed/failed/pending) or full request lifecycle states?
Do legacy sample-only score runs require separate counter schemas or a compatibility adapter?
What reconciliation cadence and alert threshold are acceptable for counter drift?
